steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Two independent clauses are joined by the conjunctive adverb "on the contrary." The rule: a semicolon must come before the conjunctive adverb, and a comma must follow it — [independent clause]; on the contrary, [independent clause].

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — rare on the contrary,: No punctuation before "on the contrary" — the two independent clauses run together. ✗
  • Choice B — rare, on the contrary;: The semicolon is placed after "on the contrary" instead of before it. The comma before it cannot join two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice C — rare, on the contrary,: A comma before "on the contrary" creates a comma splice — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice D — rare; on the contrary,: Semicolon before the conjunctive adverb, comma after. Follows the rule exactly. ✓
That matches Choice D — rare; on the contrary,.
